selection.greatFrontendReasonIfOmitted: No exact-topic GreatFrontEnd page was confirmed for this JavaScript prompt, so broader JavaScript question-bank pages would be weaker competitors than topic-direct sources.

competitors.label: MDN Classes
competitors.selectionReason: MDN is the canonical reference for class syntax, constructors, fields, and inheritance behavior in JavaScript.
theirStrengths: It is the authoritative reference for class syntax, fields, methods, and inheritance.
theirGaps: It is less compact on the practical interview point that classes are syntax over prototypes and that method sharing is the real under-the-hood behavior to understand.

realWorldUseCases: JavaScript classes are cleaner syntax over prototypes, but the practical questions are under-the-hood method sharing, field initialization, and when class-heavy modeling adds more ceremony than value.
actionableExamples: Behind the Scenes Even though classes look new, they’re still built on top of JavaScript’s **prototype chain**: - Every class has a hidden [[Prototype]] link. - The class body methods are added to ClassName.prototype. - When you call a method on an instance, JavaScript looks it up through this prototype chain. This means the following two approaches are equivalent in behavior: ```javascript function Person(name) { this.name = name; } Person.prototype.greet = function() { console.log('Hello, ' + this.name); }; ``` ```javascript class Person { constructor(name) { this.name = name; } greet() { console.log('Hello, ' + this.name); } } ```
followUpConfusion: How Classes Work Internally - The class syntax is **syntactic sugar** over constructor functions and the prototype chain. - Each class has a special constructor() method that initializes new objects. - Methods defined inside a class are automatically added to the prototype, not the instance. - This keeps memory usage efficient since all instances share the same methods.

realWorldUseCases: MDN covers classes comprehensively, but our page is more explicit about the practical interview question developers actually get: what classes add and what they do not change under the hood.
actionableExamples: The canonical docs still have strong syntax and inheritance examples, so the example criterion remains competitive.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more direct on the likely follow-up confusion that classes are syntax sugar and that methods still live on the prototype rather than on each instance.

actionableExamples: Add one shorter field-initialization example later if we need more example distance over the docs.

competitors.winReason: MDN is the canonical class reference, but our page is stronger for the interview prompt because it keeps the answer focused on classes as prototype-based syntax sugar and on shared-method behavior.

competitors.label: javascript.info Class basic syntax
competitors.selectionReason: javascript.info is a topic-direct tutorial for class syntax, methods, and the prototype-based model beneath classes.
theirStrengths: It is strong on class syntax and on explaining how classes work in practice.
theirGaps: It is less compact as an interview answer and less explicit on when class-heavy modeling adds ceremony without enough value.

realWorldUseCases: JavaScript classes are cleaner syntax over prototypes, but the practical questions are under-the-hood method sharing, field initialization, and when class-heavy modeling adds more ceremony than value.
actionableExamples: Behind the Scenes Even though classes look new, they’re still built on top of JavaScript’s **prototype chain**: - Every class has a hidden [[Prototype]] link. - The class body methods are added to ClassName.prototype. - When you call a method on an instance, JavaScript looks it up through this prototype chain. This means the following two approaches are equivalent in behavior: ```javascript function Person(name) { this.name = name; } Person.prototype.greet = function() { console.log('Hello, ' + this.name); }; ``` ```javascript class Person { constructor(name) { this.name = name; } greet() { console.log('Hello, ' + this.name); } } ```
followUpConfusion: How Classes Work Internally - The class syntax is **syntactic sugar** over constructor functions and the prototype chain. - Each class has a special constructor() method that initializes new objects. - Methods defined inside a class are automatically added to the prototype, not the instance. - This keeps memory usage efficient since all instances share the same methods.

realWorldUseCases: javascript.info is a strong class tutorial, but our page is more explicit about the trade-off question interviewers often ask next: when classes help readability and when they add ceremony.
actionableExamples: Its examples are strong and topic-direct, so the example criterion remains tied.
followUpConfusion: Our page is clearer on the key follow-up: class methods are still shared on the prototype, so class syntax does not replace the underlying object model.

actionableExamples: If needed later, add one short public-field or static-method example to create more example distance over tutorial coverage.

competitors.winReason: javascript.info is a strong classes tutorial, but our page is better calibrated for this prompt because it keeps the answer anchored to prototype-based method sharing and the cost-benefit question of class-heavy design.

competitors.label: Stack Overflow class methods vs prototype methods
competitors.selectionReason: This is a focused community Q&A source for the exact under-the-hood distinction between per-instance methods and prototype-shared methods that sits behind class syntax.
theirStrengths: It is tightly focused on shared prototype methods versus per-instance methods, which is a key class follow-up.
theirGaps: It is narrower than the full interview question and does not cover class syntax, constructors, and readability trade-offs in one answer.

realWorldUseCases: JavaScript classes are cleaner syntax over prototypes, but the practical questions are under-the-hood method sharing, field initialization, and when class-heavy modeling adds more ceremony than value.
actionableExamples: Behind the Scenes Even though classes look new, they’re still built on top of JavaScript’s **prototype chain**: - Every class has a hidden [[Prototype]] link. - The class body methods are added to ClassName.prototype. - When you call a method on an instance, JavaScript looks it up through this prototype chain. This means the following two approaches are equivalent in behavior: ```javascript function Person(name) { this.name = name; } Person.prototype.greet = function() { console.log('Hello, ' + this.name); }; ``` ```javascript class Person { constructor(name) { this.name = name; } greet() { console.log('Hello, ' + this.name); } } ```

realWorldUseCases: The thread is useful on one under-the-hood detail, but our page covers the full interview answer: syntax, prototypes, constructors, and when classes help readability.
actionableExamples: A focused Q&A still provides concrete shared-method examples, so the example criterion remains close.
followUpConfusion: Our page resolves more of the likely follow-up set in one place instead of centering only on method placement.

actionableExamples: Keep the behind-the-scenes equivalence example near the opening so the answer stays more complete than a narrow prototype-method thread.

competitors.winReason: The Stack Overflow thread is strong on shared methods, but our page is a better interview answer because it connects that detail to the wider class-as-syntax-sugar model.
